coredata.dc:title: Performance of amine-modified CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites for enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution

coredata.dc:description: The exploration of an efficient photocatalyst for H2 evolution directly from water splitting is highly desirable due to the current environmental and energy situation. The present work successfully used a solvothermal method to synthesize organic-inorganic CdS-diethylenetriamine (CdS-D) nanorods (NRs). The amine-modified CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite materials were prepared using the self-assembly method with different ZIF-8 nanocrystals (NCs) weight ratios. At λ ≥ 420 nm wavelength, the optimized CdS-D@ZIF-8 (CZ-2) nanocomposite with 5.0 wt% loading of ZIF-8 NCs showed the highest performance of 2293.9 μmol g−1 h−1 H2 evolution and an apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 4.95%. The CZ-2 nanocomposite's activity was 114.69, 5.25, and 1.32 times higher than that of ZIF-8 NCs (20.0 μmol g−1 h−1), CdS-D NRs (436.4 μmol g−1 h−1) and 1.0 wt% Pt/CdS-D (1737.3 μmol g−1 h−1), respectively. The cyclic photostability of the prepared CZ-2 nanocomposite remained unchanged after six consecutive cycles. The UV-DRS, electrochemical measurements, and Mott-Schottky (MS) analysis were performed to explain the band edge positions for CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The detailed S-scheme charge transfer mechanism of the as-prepared catalysts was also studied using the density functional theory (DFT). This work provides vital information for the controllable synthesis of ZIF-8-modified S-scheme nanocomposites for solar energy utilization.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exploration of an efficient photocatalyst for H2 evolution directly from water splitting is highly desirable due to the current environmental and energy situation. The present work successfully used a solvothermal method to synthesize organic-inorganic CdS-diethylenetriamine (CdS-D) nanorods (NRs). The amine-modified CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite materials were prepared using the self-assembly method with different ZIF-8 nanocrystals (NCs) weight ratios. At λ ≥ 420 nm wavelength, the optimized CdS-D@ZIF-8 (CZ-2) nanocomposite with 5.0 wt% loading of ZIF-8 NCs showed the highest performance of 2293.9 μmol g−1 h−1 H2 evolution and an apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 4.95%. The CZ-2 nanocomposite's activity was 114.69, 5.25, and 1.32 times higher than that of ZIF-8 NCs (20.0 μmol g−1 h−1), CdS-D NRs (436.4 μmol g−1 h−1) and 1.0 wt% Pt/CdS-D (1737.3 μmol g−1 h−1), respectively. The cyclic photostability of the prepared CZ-2 nanocomposite remained unchanged after six consecutive cycles. The UV-DRS, electrochemical measurements, and Mott-Schottky (MS) analysis were performed to explain the band edge positions for CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The detailed S-scheme charge transfer mechanism of the as-prepared catalysts was also studied using the density functional theory (DFT). This work provides vital information for the controllable synthesis of ZIF-8-modified S-scheme nanocomposites for solar energy utilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Petroleum derivatives are a significant source of energy generation that significantly contributes to climate change [1–5]. Photocatalytic water splitting could be an efficient and reliable technology to meet the energy shortfall and is an environmentally responsible alternative energy source. Solar hydrogen produced through photocatalytic water splitting has been a potential alternative to conventional fossil fuels [6–8]. Charge separation, light absorption capacity, and redox capability are essential parameters that determine the practicability of a photocatalyst in water-splitting. Developing highly efficient photocatalysts has attracted attention to energy and environmental protection [9]. The appropriate photocatalyst development for water splitting has been a worthwhile project, and the choice of the cocatalyst is also critical for developing an efficient photocatalyst in water splitting. In photocatalysis systems, noble metals such as Pt, Au, and Ru are the frequently used cocatalysts. They also provide more active sites and improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs for photocatalytic H2 evolution. The high cost and limited availability of noble metals hindered commercial and large-scale H2 evolution from photocatalytic water splitting [10–13].

Recently, cadmium sulfide (CdS) has become one of the most widely used semiconductors for visible light irradiation in photocatalytic water splitting, CO2 reduction, and environmental remediation due to its suitable band structure [14–17]. The photocorrosion and poor charge separation efficiency have restricted the applications of CdS-based photocatalysts. Many techniques have been reported to improve charge separation efficiency and suppress photocorrosion [18–22]. Different approaches have been used to modify CdS photocatalysts, including element doping and morphology regulation, noble metals loading and defect engineering [23–33]. The CdS-based heterostructures can boost photogenerated electrons-hole pair separation and photostability compared to pure CdS photocatalysts [34,35]. Du and co-workers prepared MoS2/CdS hybrid catalysts for photoreforming plastic waste into valuable products and water splitting under mild reaction conditions. This study provided an effective route of harnessing solar energy using photocatalytic oxidation of real-world plastic waste (polyethylene, polyethylene terephthalate, polylactic acid) to produce continuous hydrogen gas and valuable gaseous/liquid chemicals [36].

The organic-inorganic CdS hybrid structure is an effective strategy to enhance the efficiency of the CdS-based photocatalysts [37]. Diethylenetriamine functionalization has been used to design an organic-inorganic CdS hybrid with tunable band structures and various morphologies. The organic solvent addressed the CdS photocorrosion and visible light irradiation limitations [38–41]. Recently, the S-scheme (step-scheme) mechanism has been suggested as the ideal bonding approach in photocatalytic systems and has gained significant interest [42–44]. The S-scheme mechanism is different from the existing Z-scheme of type-II heterojunctions. When two or more semiconductors are in contact in the case of the S-scheme, photogenerated electrons spontaneously transfer from one to the other from reduction potential (RP) with a high fermi energy (Ef) and holes to oxidation potential (OP) with a small Ef and high potential through the interface, leading to the accumulation of holes at OP and depletion of electrons at RP, respectively. The transfer of photoinduced electrons in the S-scheme network also depends on the difference between the work function (ɸ) and the fermi energy. An electric field created at an interface due to the charge distribution process is necessary to obtain an efficient transfer of photocarriers [45]. Recently, Peng and co-workers prepared S-scheme Cd0.5Co0.5S/S, N–TiO2 ternary nanofibers using electro-spinning and hydrothermal methods. The prepared Cd0.5Co0.5S/S, N–TiO2 ternary hybrid showed higher hydrogen evolution performance than pristine S, N doped TiO2 nanofibers and long-term stability. TEM images showed the presence of nanocrystal-like morphology in Cd0.5Co0.5S/S, N–TiO2 nanofibers hybrid. The efficient performance is due to the hybrid effect of CoS and CdS with efficient charge transfer and separation at the interface between Cd0.5Co0.5S nanoparticles and S, N-doped TiO2 nanofibers. When the Cd0.5Co0.5S ratio exceeds 3.0 wt%, the hydrogen evolution activity falls due to excessive Cd0.5Co0.5S coverage and shielding the photoabsorption of TiO2. Aforementioned work potentially suggests S-scheme heterojunction for H2 evolution from water splitting [46].

A metal-organic framework (MOF) is an exciting class of organic-inorganic hybrid with significant specific surface area (SSA), pore volume, and tunable structure. The stable chemical and unique porous structure of MOFs make them promising alternatives to traditional heterostructures in various fields such as environmental remediation, trace metal ions, gas sensing, energy storage, catalysis, and drug delivery. Various transition metal-decorated MOFs such as ZIF-8, UiO-66, MIL-125, MIL-125-NH2, and Fe-based MOFs are used for environmental remediation [47–50]. Zeolite imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) synthesis is one of the most stable and easily controlled catalysts. Nowadays, heterostructure formation based on the ZIF-8 and semiconductors has attracted much attention. The organic ligand 2-methylimidazolium is an abundant source of π electron delocalization. The band structure (4.9–5.1 eV) limits the applications of ZIF-8 in photocatalytic water splitting, gas sensing, energy conversion, and environmental remediation [51–60]. Nowadays, heterostructure formation based on the ZIF-8 and semiconductors has attracted much attention. However, studies of ZIF-8 and semiconductor hybrids for visible-light-driven photocatalytic H2 evolution are infrequent. Wu et al. prepared a diethylenetriamine-modified ternary hybrid (UiO-66/CdS/RGO) with 13.8 times higher H2 evolution activity than pure CdS nanomaterials [61]. In 2018, Lin and his team prepared a ZIF-8@g-C3N4 hybrid for photocatalytic H2 evolution and performed significantly higher H2 evolution activity compared to pristine g-C3N4 [62]. Zeng and co-workers prepared polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) stabilized CdS@ZIF-8 heterojunction and applied it for photocatalytic H2 evolution from formic acid using Pt as cocatalysts. TEM images showed the core-shell-like morphology for CdS@ZIF-8 heterojunction. The prepared CdS@ZIF-8 hybrid showed better photocurrent stability than the pristine CdS catalyst [63]. Up to date, no study has been reported for an efficient CdS-diethylenetriamine (CdS-D) modified CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite material for visible light photocatalytic H2 evolution from water splitting without the utilization of noble metals as cocatalysts.

Inspired by previous Z-scheme nanocomposites, we developed a series of novel S-scheme CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite materials using a self-assembly approach (Scheme 1 ). The ZIF-8 nanocrystals (NCs) were dispersed uniformly on the CdS-D nanorods (NRs) surface. The metal-organic framework (ZIF-8) incorporation in CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites is widely anticipated due to their high specific surface area (SSA), controlled morphologies, and tunable photoabsorption capability. The CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8) nanocomposite exhibits superior hydrogen evolution performance at λ ≥ 420 nm wavelength compared to pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The presence of ZIF-8 NCs significantly reduced the charge recombination, proved by the photoluminescence, electrochemical impendence, and photocurrent response. The first principle method was applied to calculate the band structure and density of states (DOS) for pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. Finally, a possible S-scheme reaction mechanism for the CZ-2 nanocomposite was proposed based on the electrostatic potential and fermi level of pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The synergetic effect between MOF, semiconductor catalysts, and S-scheme nanocomposites can considerably improve photocatalytic performance.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn (NO3)2·6H2O), 2-methylimidazole (CH3C2H2N2H), cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd (CH3COO)2·2H2O), thiourea (H2NCSNH2), methanol (MeOH), dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH), diethylenetriamine (DETA), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O), and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3). All chemical reagents are of analytical grade.

CdS-D nanorods (NRs) were prepared solvothermally. Typically, 1.20 g of cadmium acetate dihydrate and 0.40 g of thiourea dissolved in 36 mL DETA and 14 mL distilled water. The prepared solution is stirred for 2 h to form a clear solution, then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) for a 24 h reaction at 180 °C. Finally, the CdS-D NRs were collected after centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min), washed multiple times with deionized water and EtOH, and dried in a vacuum dryer for 12 h. The traditional CdS was also prepared without DETA solution and categorized as CdS nanoparticles (NPs).

ZIF-8 nanocrystals (NCs) were prepared following the previous research work with optimized modifications [64]. Solutions A and B are prepared separately with 0.07 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate and 0.08 g of 2-methylimidazole in 50 mL of MeOH solution. The prepared clear solutions were mixed rapidly and stirred for 30 min at 25 °C. The formed milky solution was aged for 24 h at 25 °C. As the last step, the milky ZIF-8 product was obtained after centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and washing with DMF and MeOH, followed by drying at 60 °C overnight in a vacuum dryer.

The self-assembled CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were synthesized at ambient conditions, and the synthesis procedure is shown in Scheme 1. Typically, a certain amount of the previously prepared CdS-D NRs added to 25 mL of MeOH using sonication to form a suitable dispersion (Solution C). After that, 0.07 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate was dissolved in 50 mL of MeOH and mixed with Solution C for 30 min at 25 °C to obtain Solution D. Then, after dissolving 0.08 g of 2-methylimidazole in 50 mL of MeOH (Solution E), Solutions D and E are ultrasound at 25 °C for 5 min to ensure uniform dispersion. The clear mixed solution is allowed to stand for 24 h at 25 °C under stirring. Finally, the light-yellow product is obtained after centrifugation (rotational speed: 10,000 rpm, 10 min), washing with DMF and MeOH solutions, and drying at 60 °C overnight in a vacuum oven. The prepared CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was named CZ-3, based on the 8.0 wt% of ZIF-8 NCs. The other CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were denoted as CZ-1 and CZ-2 using 3.0 and 5.0 wt% of ZIF-8 NCs, respectively.

Powder X-ray diffractometer (Model: Rigaku D/Max 2400) was used to record crystallographic information and the crystal phase of samples. The XRD patterns were collected using nickel-filtered Cu Kα radiation in 2θ degrees of 5°–80° at a scanning rate of 5°/min, at 45 kV acceleration voltage, and 200 mA current. The functional groups were determined using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR, Equinox 55). The crystal structure was further evaluated using Raman spectroscopy (InVia Qontor). A thermogravimetric differential thermal analyzer (Q600, TA instruments) was used to test the thermal stability of the prepared catalysts. Scanning electron microscopy (Nano-SEM 450) was used to observe the morphology of all the synthesized specimens. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, F30, S-twin equipment) and scanning TEM (STEM-EDX) were performed at an operational voltage of 300 kV. High-resolution TEM was used to determine the crystal lattice spacing of as-prepared photocatalysts. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, model: AVIO 500) was performed to investigate the bulk elements. The electrochemical characteristics (Photocurrent, EIS, and Mott–Schottky) were recorded using a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI650E, B20036). The UV–Vis absorbance and band structure were recorded using a JASCO UV–Vis 550 spectrophotometer. The Photoluminescence spectra were measured using Hitachi FL-4500 equipment. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms estimated specific surface area (SSA) using a surface area and porosity analyzer (Micrometritics Tristar II 3020). The valence state and chemical composition were determined employing XPS using Mg Kα radiation (Multilab2000).

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution tests were conducted in a gas-closed quartz glass reaction system coupled to a thermostat water bath to maintain the reactor at 25 °C. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution was performed with a 300 W Xenon lamp (CEL-PF300-T8) at λ ≥ 420 nm. In short, 3.0 mg of the prepared samples were dissolved in a 7.0 mL mixture of Na2S and Na2SO3, each with 0.1 M concentration and then sonicated for 15 min to develop a good dispersion. Before visible light irradiation, the reaction system was degassed for 30 min using Ar gas flow to remove the residual air. The reaction system was stirred with a magnetic stirrer (450 rpm) to keep the sample well suspended. The thermostat was attached to keep the temperature of the reaction system at 25 °C using fresh water circulation. The amount of generated H2 gas was evaluated by a 7890 Agilent gas chromatography (GC) equipped with a TCD detector and packed column system. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) was measured using monochromatic light filters with a wide range of wavelengths. The separation rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs was calculated by AQY as follows:

Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed to investigate the photogenerated charge and photocatalytic mechanisms [65,66]. The Pt foil (2 × 2 cm2) and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes. The three-electrode system was filled with a 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution (pH = 6) and used as an aqueous electrolyte. The photocurrent studies were performed at 25 °C with a 300 W Xenon lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm). The working electrode was prepared as follows: 5.0 mg of the as-prepared sample was ultrasonically dissolved in 2.0 mL of absolute ethanol for 10 min, and then 50 μL of a 5% Nafion polymer solution was dissolved in the above ethanol solution and sonicated for a further 10 min to form a clear solution. 50 μL of the prepared sample solution was dipped onto the exposed area (1 × 1 cm2) of glassy carbon substrate and allowed to dry completely in an electric oven at 60 °C. The photocurrent response was recorded at 1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl with a dark/illumination interval of 20 s. The electrochemical impedance (EIS) was recorded at 5.0 mV AC amplitude and a frequency of 1–100 kHz.

XRD patterns were recorded to determine the crystallographic information and crystal phase of as-prepared photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the prominent characteristic diffractions of CdS-D NRs were approximately at 2θ = 24.7°, 26.6°, 28.1°, 37.1°, 43.8°, 47.9°, 51.9°, 67.5°, 70.9°, and 75.8°. They could be attributed to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), (203), (211), and (105) planes of hexagonal P63mc (186) CdS (JCPDS Card No. 41–1049) without additional peaks. As shown in Fig. S1, the XRD peaks for traditional CdS NPs showed a nearly higher crystallinity than DETA-modified CdS-D NRs. The major diffractions of ZIF-8 NCs were at 2θ = 7.2°, 10.2°, 12.6°, 14.7°, 16.4°, 17.9°, 19.5°, 22.1°, 24.6°, 26.8°, 29.7°, and 30.6°, and could be attributed to (001), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (123), (114), (233), (134), (044), and (244) planes as shown in Fig. 1(b), respectively. All the significant peaks of ZIF-8 NCs and CdS-D NRs were observed in the crystalline phase of CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites, which predicted the successful hybridization of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. However, ZIF-8 NCs peak intensities were poor, owing to the lower mass fraction of ZIF-8 in the CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite [67]. As the amount of pure ZIF-8 NCs increases, the peaks from CZ-1 to CZ-3 are strengthened gradually [68]. The ZIF-8 NCs modification did not change the crystallographic information and crystal phases of pristine CdS-D NRs. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the FT-IR spectrum was applied to investigate further the structural constituents of pure CdS-D NRs, ZIF-8 NCs, and their CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The absorption peaks of CdS-D NRs were recorded at 3234.1 and 1654.5 cm−1, indicating the presence of DETA and that poor intensity was due to the small amount of DETA used in the synthesis of CdS-D NRs [69]. The antisymmetric stretching vibrations of saturated C–H (CH3) and C–H (CH2) hydrocarbons were recorded at 2978.0 and 2929.8 cm−1, respectively. The CN bond imidazole ring stretching was registered at 1587.4 cm−1. The absorption peaks for methyl flexural vibration were recorded at 1450.7 and 1382.1 cm−1. The corresponding C–N stretching vibrations were 1143.7 and 995.2 cm−1. In addition, the equivalent peak at 759.9 cm−1 belonged to Zn–O stretching vibration, and the peak at 421.3 cm−1 fit into Zn–N stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectra confirmed the successful hybridization of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs in CZ-1 (3.0 wt% ZIF-8), CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8), and CZ-3 (8.0 wt% ZIF-8) nanocomposites.

According to Fig. S2(a), two Raman peaks of CdS-D NRs were attributed to the first-order (1LO) and second-order (2LO) vibrational modes. The variation in intensity and Raman peaks are attributed to the change in crystallinity. The first (1LO) and second-order (2LO) peaks of pristine CdS-D NRs were located at 300 and 601 cm−1, respectively [70,71]. The figure shows four prominent vibrational peaks at 678, 1020, 1141, and 1454 cm−1 were observed for ZIF-8 NCs, corresponding to imidazole ring, ν C5–N, and δ C–H, respectively. The following vibrational modes were according to the literature for ZIF-8 NCs [72]. The low intensity of the characteristic peak of ZIF-8 NCs was observed in the CZ-2 nanocomposite, owing to the high baseline generated by the low content of ZIF-8 NCs. The enlarged spectra for the CZ-2 nanocomposite in Fig. S2(b) indicated the successful growth of ZIF-8 NCs on the surface of CdS-D NRs. The shift in vibrational modes was related to decreased stretching frequency and indicated the successful growth of CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. A 42.6% weight loss was recorded for pristine ZIF-8 NCs, as shown in Fig. S3. In addition, the pure CdS-D NRs showed a total weight loss of 12.3%, indicating good thermal stability. The CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites showed similar TGA curves to those of CdS-D NRs. Moreover, the total weight losses for CdS-D, CZ-1, CZ-2, and CZ-3 nanocomposites were about 12.3, 15.4, 16.5, and 20.8%, respectively. There was no significant difference between the actual and the experimental weight ratio of deposited ZIF-8 NCs on CdS-D NRs.

The optical properties, including UV–vis absorbance, steady-state photocurrent response, EIS, and photoluminescence (PL), are shown in Fig. 4 (a–c) and Fig. S9. The pure CdS-D NRs had strong photoabsorption in the UV–vis region and absorbed up to 550 nm wavelength, as shown in Fig. 4(a). Moreover, the milky-colored ZIF-8 NCs have strong photoabsorption in the UV region and limited visible light absorption capability. Because of the low mass fraction of ZIF-8, there was very little change in the UV–vis DRS spectra of CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites and only a slight decrease in photoabsorption with an increasing amount of ZIF-8 NCs. The photocurrent experiments were intermittently carried out at 25 °C at λ ≥ 420 nm for 20 s intervals in Fig. 4(b). The higher photocurrent response of CZ-2 indicated better photoinduced electron-hole separation efficiency than pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. EIS was performed to investigate further influential inner aspects of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 4(c) were created using the Zview 2 software with Rs (RctCPE) circuit model, where Rs represents the solution resistance, Rct for interfacial charge transfer resistance between the working electrode and the electrolyte, and CPE is the constant phase element. As a result, the semicircle radii represent charge transfer at the interface of catalyst and electrolyte, with the smaller radius indicating lower resistance [75]. The possible equivalent circuit model is given in Fig. S8 As shown in Table S2, the Rct value for the CZ-2 nanocomposite is much lower than the pristine ZIF-8 NCs, and CdS-D NRs. The lower Rct indicated fast charge transfer at the interface of the catalyst and electrolyte, which can efficiently control the charge recombination and improve performance [76]. The Rct value of the CZ-2 nanocomposite was much lower than that of pure ZIF-8 NCs and CdS-D NRs, indicating a preferable charge transfer efficiency in the CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. The low resistance to charge separation and transfer for CZ-2 has enhanced electron-hole separation and H2 evolution performance. The steady-state PL analysis was used to determine the charge recombination rate and electron-hole separation efficiency for CdS-D NRs, ZIF-8 NCs, and their heterojunctions, as shown in Fig. S9. The PL spectra for CdS-D NRs, ZIF-8 NCs, CZ-1 (3.0 wt% ZIF-8), CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8), and CZ-3 (8.0 wt% ZIF-8) nanocomposites was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The lower peak spectra indicate good separation efficiency, while the high peak intensity indicates poor separation efficiency [24]. When CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs were combined, the peak intensity decreased compared to pure catalysts. The peak intensity of CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8) was considerably less than pure CdS-D NRs when loading ZIF-8 NCs on the surface of CdS-D NRs, indicating that the electrons on the conduction band of CdS-D NRs transfer to the surface of ZIF-8 NCs effectively. Adding ZIF-8 NCs on the surface of CdS-D NRs effectively improved the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes while reducing their recombination. As a result, the CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite with 5.0 wt% ZIF-8 NCs displayed the prolonged separation ability of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and boosted interfacial charge transfer efficiency, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, which is consistent with FTIR, DRS, and EIS results.

The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 5 (a) for CdS-D, ZIF-8, and CZ-2 nanocomposite revealed the dominant peaks of Cd, S, C, N, and Zn elements. Zn 2p spectra were visible in both ZIF-8 NCs and CZ-2 nanocomposite. Moreover, Cd 3d peaks in CdS-D NRs and CZ-2 indicated that the CZ-2 nanocomposites were synthesized successfully. Furthermore, the Cd 3d spectra for CdS-D and CZ-2 are shown in Fig. 5(b), demonstrating that the ZIF-8 NCs were decorated on CdS-D NRs. The binding energy (BE) peaks of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 for CdS-D NRs were located at 405.0 and 411.75 eV, respectively, according to Fig. 5(b). The Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 spectra in the CZ-2 at 404.45 and 411.15 eV, respectively, shifted toward lower BE due to the interaction between CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. According to Fig. 5(c), S 2p spectra for CdS-D NRs assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 were at 160.45 and 161.67 eV BE, respectively. In comparison, S 2p spectra for CZ-2 nanocomposite were fitted at the BE level of 160.63 and 161.75 eV and attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, suggesting the presence of S2⁻ states [77]. The Zn 2p spectra for ZIF-8 NCs are shown in Fig. 5(d), with the distinct doublets at 1021.70 and 1044.60 eV BE levels assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of Zn2⁺ state. The Zn 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 spectra for the CZ-2 nanocomposite have lower BE of 1021.30 and 1044.10 eV, respectively. The change in BE indicated that Zn oxidation was reduced due to CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite formation, which increased Zn electron density. Fig. 5(e) shows that C 1s spectra for ZIF-8 NCs were deconvoluted into three sub-peaks at 284.60, 286.18, and 288.5 eV, which could be accredited to C–C, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively. The C 1s spectra for CZ-2 nanocomposite at 284.70, 286.23, and 288.57 eV BE levels were ascribed to C–C, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively [78]. The N 1s spectra for ZIF-8 NCs in Fig. 5(f) revealed a single peak at 398.85 eV, assigned to the 2-methylimidazole ligand. The N 1s spectra for CZ-2 nanocomposite showed two peaks due to the addition of CdS-D NRs. The 399.05 eV and 404.30 eV BE peaks correspond to the imidazole ring (CN bond) of ZIF-8 NCs and nitrate groups of CdS-D NRs [79]. The variations in the peak position of C 1s and N 1s BE could be because of the surface coupling effect through heterojunction formation [80]. As a result, the XPS results confirmed the successful formation of CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The BE levels of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs are consistent with the reported results [81,82]. The atomic composition and BE comparison of as-prepared photocatalysts are summarised in Table S3 and Table S4.

The porous characteristics and band structure for CdS-D NRs, ZIF-8 NCs, and CZ-2 nanocomposite are summarised in Table S5. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and SSA are represented in Fig. S10. The SSA of CdS-D NRs, CZ-1, CZ-2, and CZ-3, ZIF-8 NCs are 21.03, 67.63, 155.97, 211.35, and 1338.51 m2/g, respectively. The SSA of CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites was much higher than that of pure CdS-D NRs. It is supposed that the composite formation prevented aggregation by decreasing the interaction points and improving the specific active sites of CdS-D NRs. ZIF-8 NCs were suitable adsorbents and could improve photocatalytic H2 evolution by improving charge separation and transformation [83].

The photocatalytic H2 generation reaction was carried out under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm), as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The photocatalytic H2 evolution from water splitting was studied using CdS NPs, CdS-D NRs, ZIF-8 NCs, and CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The CdS-D NRs had a higher H2 evolution performance of 436.4 μmol g−1 h−1 than traditional CdS NPs (242.2 μmol g−1 h−1). The amine-modified linkage between inorganic catalysts prevents the formation of aggregation and thus provides more active sites for photocatalytic water reduction. DETA modulated the band structure of CdS, unlike other conventional surfactants and ammonium protonation reduced charge diffusion distance [84–86]. The weak photoabsorption of ZIF-8 NCs may be due to the vast band structure, poor charge separation, and poor transfer. The different amounts of ZIF-8 NCs (3, 5, and 8 wt%) incorporation in the CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites showed significantly improved H2 evolution. It should be noted that the CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites should contain a sufficient amount of ZIF-8 NCs. The excess ZIF-8 on the CdS-D surface served as an electron and hole recombination centre [87]. The H2 evolution performance of numerous reported CdS-based catalysts is shown in Table S6. The CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8) nanocomposite exhibits the highest H2 evolution activity of 2293.9 μmol g−1 h−1. In addition, photoabsorption wavelength and photocatalytic H2 evolution activity were further decreased while increasing the ZIF-8 content (8.0 wt% ZIF-8). The CZ-2 nanocomposite with a ZIF-8 NCs cocatalyst outperformed the Pt/CdS-D (1.0 wt% Pt) by 1.32 times.

The cyclic stability experiments were performed to evaluate the long-term reusability and reproducibility of the CZ-2 nanocomposite for H2 evolution. After six consecutive cycles (4 h cycle) at λ ≥ 420 nm, the CZ-2 nanocomposite almost had an insignificant change in photocatalytic H2 evolution (Fig. 6(b)). The construction of a CdS-D@ZIF-8 heterojunction benefited the electron-hole separation and retained the specific redox capability compared to pristine ZIF-8 NCs and CdS-D NRs. It showed that adding a sufficient amount of ZIF-8 can considerably increase the photocatalytic stability of CdS-DETA and its photocatalytic activity. In addition, Fig. S11 showed the XRD patterns of the CZ-2 nanocomposite before and after the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution test. It was confirmed that there was no discernible difference in characteristic XRD diffractions, indicating the strong photostability of the CZ-2 nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 6, the UV–vis absorbance wavelength and intensity of the irradiating light played a significant role in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. As a result, it is essential to report AQY values in combination with the intensity and transmission of incident light [88]. The AQY was measured using several monochromatic light filters with 420, 500, 520, and 550 nm wavelengths. The curve of the AQY value (blue point plot) increased significantly but decreased from 500 nm, which corresponded well with the absorption spectra under incident light intensity. The AQY reached about 4.95% for CZ-2 nanocomposite at λ ≥ 420 nm, while at λ ≥ 520 nm wavelength, it showed an AQY of 0.42% (Fig. 6(c)).

The catalyst dosage significantly influences photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The optimal sample dosage was estimated with catalyst loadings ranging from 0 to 5.0 mg for hydrogen evolution. Fig. S12 described the influence of catalyst dose on hydrogen evolution employing CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8) nanocomposite with an S-scheme. The hydrogen evolution rate for the catalyst with 1.0 mg loading was 1377.4 μmol g−1 h−1. When the catalyst dose reached 3.0 mg, the hydrogen evolution rate increased to 2293.9 μmol g−1 h−1. The loading content of the photocatalyst determines the density of active sites and light absorption capacity to regulate the hydrogen evolution reaction. The higher hydrogen evolution suggests that more active sites and high photon flux generation enhanced the occurrence of redox reactions [89]. The hydrogen evolution rate decreased to 1929.7 μmol g−1 h−1 with a catalyst dosage of 5.0 mg. The decrease in activity with enhanced catalyst dose is due to the light shielding effect to generate appropriate photon flux, photocatalyst particle aggregation, and commencing electron-hole recombination, which is critical for hydrogen evolution. It is confirmed that evaluating the ideal catalyst loading is essential to develop better interaction between catalyst and solar spectrum to improve the formation and separation of photoinduced electron and hole pairs to initiate the redox reactions, which is consistent with literature results [90].

Tauc plots from UV–vis absorbance data and Mott–Schottky (MS) were used to characterize the band positions of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The bandgap energies for CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs were calculated to be 2.25 and 4.90 eV, respectively (Fig. 7 (a)). The semiconductor type and flat band positions of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs are determined using MS plots. The flat-band potential (Vfb) is the difference between the water reduction potential and the fermi level. According to the intercept with the potential axis in Fig. 7(b), the Vfb of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs were calculated to be −0.91, and −0.42 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in sodium sulfate solution and the positive slopes of MS plots revealed the n-type semiconductor nature of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The CdS-D NRs have more negative Vfb than ZIF-8 NCs, indicating a more negative conduction band position for CdS-D NRs. As the conduction band potential of an n-type photocatalyst is to be 0.1–0.2 V lower than the Vfb, the potential difference is set to be 0.2 V in this study [91]. EVB and ECB denote the potential edges of the VB and CB, respectively. An empirical formula
(
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g
) was used to calculate EVB and ECB in CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The ECB and EVB of CdS-D NRs are estimated to be −0.71 V and 1.54 V vs. NHE, respectively, while those for ZIF-8 NCs were −0.22 V and 4.68 V [92–94]. The Z-scheme, type II, or S-scheme could be the potential reaction mechanism for improved hydrogen evolution performance over CZ-2 (3.0 wt% ZIF-8 NCs) catalyst. However, recent research has shown that the Z-scheme mechanism suffers from inevitable scientific differences and is problematic due to its inherent limitations [95]. The S-scheme or type-II reaction mechanism can explain the improved hydrogen evolution performance of the CdS-D@ZIF-8 heterojunction. First, we consider the potential of a type-II photocatalytic mechanism. Further investigation revealed that the ECB of ZIF-8 NCs (−0.22 eV vs. NHE) is more positive than the reduction potential for hydrogen evolution (H+/H2 = 0.41 eV vs. NHE) [96]. As a result, electrons on the conduction band of ZIF-8 NCs could not convert H+ to generate hydrogen gas. Because of this, the type-II reaction mechanism is limited in our study. Thus, CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs have matched with the S-scheme energy band structure, facilitating photocarrier transfer [97–99]. The charge transfer path must be clarified to establish the photocatalytic hydrogen generation mechanism between CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The band edge positions of pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs are shown in Fig. 7(c). CdS-D was more likely to be activated by visible light than ZIF-8 due to a significant difference in the forbidden bandwidth, confirmed by hydrogen evolution performance.

The GGA-PBE and hybrid-B3LYP functionals were used to optimize the geometry for pristine ZIF-8 NCs and CdS-D NRs, as shown in Figs. S12(a–b). Calculating a semiconductor band structure is critical for determining the band gap, energy band nature, and dispersion of the bands. These features are advantageous for photocatalysis and optoelectronic devices such as photodetectors and solar cells [88]. Fig. 8(a–d) showed the predicted band structures and total density of states (TDOS) of pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. A dotted black line marked the Fermi level. It was found that the ZIF-8 NCs had low electrical conductivity due to their vast band structure. The conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs are located near the G points. The direct band gap semiconductor had an excellent optical absorption capacity than the indirect band gap semiconductor because phonons were required for the indirect transition of electrons [100]. The band structure of CdS-D NRs derived from GGA-PBE functionals is substantially lower than those of experimental values from UV–vis-DRS because GGA-PBE exchange-correlation functionals have flaws in modelling the physical properties of CdS-D NRs [101]. CdS-D is being evaluated using a variety of exchange-correlation functionals. The hybrid-B3LYP exchange-correlation functional generated a 2.30 eV band gap for CdS-D NRs, close to the experimental value (2.25 eV). The band gaps obtained from other functionals are computed in Table S7. As shown in Fig. 8 (c), The calculated band structure for ZIF-8 NCs is also close to the experimental value (4.90 eV).

DOS explains how state occupancy changes over certain energy intervals [102]. The PDOS plots for the pristine ZIF-8 NCs and CdS-D NRs are shown in Fig. S14. The dotted lines in both plots indicated the fermi energy level of the pristine catalysts. PDOS graphs demonstrated electron transitions from the valence to conduction bands. The computed PDOS for CdS-D NRs is presented in Fig. S14(a-b), respectively. In S PDOS, S s-states dominated the first region between 13.5 and 11.9 eV energy. The s, p, and d-states of Cd PDOS had low-energy peaks. The energy band between −8.2 eV and −5.3 eV was occupied by Cd d-states. Cd s-states and S p-states dominated the third energy band between 5.2 and 0.1 eV. The conduction band influenced Cd s-states and S p-states. The conduction band was primarily composed of Cd s-states and S p-states. Cd s-states can be seen clearly during conduction because they tend to be at the lowest points of the conduction band. The computed PDOS for ZIF-8 NCs is presented in Figs. S14(c–f). Energy bands ranging from −20 to −10 eV dominated the 1s and 2s states of H, C, and N. The 1s state of H, 2s, and 2p states of N and C atoms and the 3d state of Zn atoms hybridized in the upper part of VB for ZIF-8. The hybridization in the upper part of VB indicated covalent bonds on the imidazole ring and a coordination bond between the Zn and N atoms. There is a continuous structure to the conduction band. The strong peak in CB is assumed to be caused by the hybridization of C and N atoms in 2p-states.

A possible reaction mechanism is proposed according to electrostatic potential and fermi levels of CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. Fig. S15 displayed the optimized structures of CdS-D NRs (1 0 1) and ZIF-8 NCs (0 0 1) surfaces. The electrostatic potential of CdS-D NRs (1 0 1) and ZIF-8 NCs (0 0 1) is calculated after geometry optimization to obtain their work functions. The work function (ɸ) is defined as ɸ = Evac-EF, where Evac and EF are the vacuum and fermi energies of CdS NRs and ZIF-8 NCs, respectively. The calculated work function for CdS-D NRs (1 0 1) and ZIF-8 NCs (0 0 1) planes are 4.82 and 5.19 eV, respectively (Fig. 9 (a) and b). The fermi energy of the CdS-D NRs (1 0 1) plane is higher than that of ZIF-8 NCs (0 0 1). Moreover, during preparation, electrons migrate from a photocatalyst with a higher Fermi level to a photocatalyst with a lower Fermi level. A possible charge transfer mechanism is also proposed in Fig. 10 according to UV-DRS spectra, MS plots, and electrostatic potentials. CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs were excited to produce photocarriers after light illumination. The photoinduced electrons at ZIF-8 NCs moved to the ZIF-8/CdS-D interface and perished along with the photoinduced holes at CdS-D NRs due to the synergistic action of the internal electric field. As a result, strong reducing electrons at CdS-D NRs and strong oxidizing holes at ZIF-8 NCs preserved to participate in the redox processes. The electrons generated in the CB of CdS-D NRs are capable of reducing H2O and producing H+ and H2 [103]. In the meantime, the sacrificial agent consumed the preserved holes in the VB of ZIF-8 NCs. The amine modification improved electrons-holes separation efficiency for CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites, which corresponds well to PL results [104]. The CZ-2 nanocomposite provided improved conductivity and better charge transfer than pristine ZIF-8 and CdS-D. Moreover, the ZIF-8 modification can enhance the electron trapping capability and photocatalytic H2 evolution of CdS-D-derived nanocomposites.

In summary, CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were prepared using a self-assembly strategy. Various characterizations were performed to confirm the development of ZIF-8 NCs on the surface of CdS-D nanorods NRs. The CZ-2 (5.0 wt% ZIF-8 of CdS-D@ZIF-8) nanocomposite offers a tunable band gap, suitable photoabsorption, efficient spatial electron-hole separation, and smooth carrier transport. Photocatalytic H2 evolution was performed from 420 to 550 nm with an AQY of 4.95% at λ ≥ 420 nm and 0.42% at λ ≥ 550 nm. The CZ-2 nanocomposite showed the highest H2 evolution performance (λ ≥ 420 nm) of 2293.9 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 5.20 and 114.69 times more than the pristine CdS-D NRs and ZIF-8 NCs. The CdS-D NRs provided more active sites, improved performance, and created strong interaction with ZIF-8 NCs. The CZ-2 nanocomposite showed excellent cyclic stability and recyclability. The photocatalysis process described in this paper emphasizes the importance of the CdS-D@ZIF-8 nanocomposite for photocatalytic material synthesis and the need for noble metals-free catalysts. Meanwhile, it is another example of using noble metals-free modified nanocomposites for S-scheme photocatalytic hydrogen evolution systems in sustainable energy development. The novel preparation scheme can provide many ideas for developing semiconductor/MOFs heterojunction and modifying the H2 evolution and cyclic stability for catalysis, environmental remediation, and sustainable energy conversion.
